summary.brief: Davis v. Mercy Medical Center. Medical malpractice case involving emergency medicine.

evidence_index.text: In February 2016, Michael Davis visited CHI in Williston. Cherise Norby, N.P., treated Davis for flu-like symptoms.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Davis returned to Norby in October 2016, complaining of frothy urine. Blood and urine tests showed Davis had an elevated white blood cell count and protein and blood in his urine.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: In June 2017, Davis followed up with Norby. Davis’ lab results showed the blood and protein levels in his urine had tripled since the earlier tests.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On September 14, 2017, Davis went to the emergency room at Holy Rosary Healthcare in Miles City, MT, complaining of hand and leg pain.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On March 29, 2018, Davis had a renal biopsy at Mayo Clinic. The biopsy demonstrated Davis was in kidney failure because of a kidney disease known as IgA nephropathy.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Denker testified IgA nephropathy is treatable, and the earlier you treat it, the better.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Bradley Denker

evidence_index.text: Dr. Denker testified that if Davis’ kidney disease was treated earlier, his kidney failure could have been delayed by approximately 15 years.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Bradley Denker

evidence_index.text: The jury found Norby was not at fault in her treatment of Michael Davis. The jury found Dr. Keene was at fault in his treatment of Davis, and Dr. Keene’s fault was a proximate cause of the Davises’ injuries.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The district court denied the motion and upheld the jury’s verdict.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Viewing the evidence in the light most favorable to the verdict, a jury could have found Dr. Keene’s failure to refer Michael Davis to a nephrologist was the proximate cause of Davis’ injuries.
evidence_index.speaker: Court reasoning

evidence_index.text: Davis authenticated the medical bills and payment summaries sufficient for the district court to determine the jury could find the documents reflected what Davis purported them to be—documents showing his medical expenses.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: We remand for an appropriate reduction of the jury’s award of past economic damages to Michael Davis.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The jury awarded Michael Davis $1,100,000 in future economic damages.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The court abused its discretion. We reverse the Davises’ award of disbursements and costs and remand for further proceedings.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

initial_state.chief_complaint: Frothy urine and elevated white blood cell count
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient initially presented with flu-like symptoms in February 2016. In October 2016, he complained of frothy urine. Blood and urine tests showed elevated white blood cell count and protein and blood in urine.

focused_exam.general: Flu-like symptoms initially, later frothy urine

requestables.name: Blood and Urine Tests
requestables.description: Tests showing elevated white blood cell count, protein, and blood in urine

reveal.result_summary: Elevated white blood cell count, protein, and blood in urine

requestables.name: Urologist Consultation
requestables.description: Consultation with urologist who found no urological explanation

reveal.result_summary: No urological explanation for abnormal test results
reveal.decision_impact: Should have led to nephrology referral

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with flu-like symptoms and later with frothy urine

key_events.event: Follow-up with frothy urine and abnormal tests

timeline_phases.name: Further Evaluation
timeline_phases.description: Patient undergoes further testing and consultations

key_events.event: Emergency room visit with abnormal labs

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with abnormal blood and urine tests
decision_points.prompt: Should the patient be referred to a nephrologist?

options.clinical_reasoning: Abnormal test results indicate possible kidney disease requiring specialist evaluation
Defendant did not refer despite abnormal results

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant did not refer to nephrologist

expected_action_court.description: Referral to nephrologist should have been made
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated early referral is critical

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to refer led to delayed diagnosis and treatment
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Referral to nephrologist for early intervention
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed kidney failure and required a transplant
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Chronic kidney disease requiring lifelong management

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to refer to nephrologist
Causation established - earlier referral could have delayed kidney failure

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Early referral could have delayed kidney failure

factual_timeline.event: Patient presented with frothy urine and abnormal tests
Patient visited ER with hand and leg pain

tests_performed.result: Elevated white blood cell count, protein, and blood in urine

court_findings.finding: Dr. Keene breached duty of care
